seasonal_wine.name: Saint-Vincent winegrowers feast, late January
seasonal_wine.description: On and around 22 January, growers across Champagne villages honour Saint Vincent, patron of winemakers, with costumed parades, a mass and a glass shared while the vines rest. It falls just as winter pruning begins, a thanksgiving for the past harvest and a blessing for the next.
seasonal_wine.tip: Each year the grand Archiconfrerie parade moves to a different Champagne town; check which village or city hosts before you travel.

seasonal_wine.name: Winter pruning, January to March
seasonal_wine.description: Through the cold months growers prune the dormant vines by hand under the four authorised systems, Chablis, Cordon de Royat, Guyot and Vallee de la Marne. The cut wood sets the yield and balance of the coming vintage, so pruning is among the most skilled jobs in the calendar.
seasonal_wine.tip: Late winter is the quietest, cheapest time to visit; many houses run relaxed tastings without the harvest crowds.

seasonal_wine.name: Budburst and spring frost watch, April
seasonal_wine.description: Budburst in April is Champagne's most anxious moment, as tender green shoots are exposed to late frosts that can wipe out a crop. Growers light candles, run wind machines and spray water to protect the buds on cold clear nights.
seasonal_wine.tip: If you see frost candles glowing in the vineyards at dawn in April, it means a cold night threatened the young buds.

seasonal_wine.name: Flowering of the vines, June
seasonal_wine.description: The vines flower in June, and growers count roughly one hundred days from flowering to the start of harvest. A clean, even flowering in warm dry weather is the first strong signal of a generous, healthy vintage to come.
seasonal_wine.tip: June visits pair vineyard walks among the flowering vines with the Flaneries Musicales concerts in Reims.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison and ripening, August
seasonal_wine.description: In August the grapes change colour and soften at veraison as sugar climbs and acidity falls. The Comite Champagne monitoring network samples 450 parcels weekly to track maturity and acidity, the data that will fix the harvest start dates.
seasonal_wine.tip: Watch the regional press in late August for the maturity reports that hint at how early the vendange will open.

seasonal_wine.name: Ban des vendanges, the harvest, September
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, late August to early October
seasonal_wine.description: Harvest in Champagne is by hand to keep the grapes whole, with more than 120,000 pickers in the vineyards each year. The Comite Champagne proclaims the ban des vendanges, a village-by-village start date, usually in September, though recent warm years have opened in late August.
seasonal_wine.tip: Some estates offer harvest-experience days; book months ahead because beds and pickers fill fast in the vendange weeks.

seasonal_wine.name: Pressing and vins clairs, autumn
seasonal_wine.description: Pressed gently in whole bunches, the juice ferments into still base wines, the vins clairs, through autumn. Each parcel and variety is kept separate so the cellar master has the widest palette of components for the spring blend.
seasonal_wine.tip: A handful of growers let visitors taste raw vins clairs in autumn, a rare look at Champagne before it sparkles.

seasonal_wine.name: Champagne Day, late October
seasonal_wine.description: On the fourth Friday of October, the global Champagne Day sees bars, shops and houses pour bubbles and share the hashtag worldwide. The Comite Champagne hosts the official event directory, with tastings across Reims and Epernay.
seasonal_wine.tip: Follow the official Champagne Day events map to find tastings and open cellars on the day across the region.

seasonal_wine.name: Blending and assemblage, winter
seasonal_wine.description: Over winter the cellar master assembles the blend from dozens of vins clairs and reserve wines held back from earlier years. This assemblage is the art at the heart of non-vintage Champagne, designed to taste consistent year after year.
seasonal_wine.tip: Ask a house how much reserve wine sits in their non-vintage blend; the deepest reserves give the most complex Brut.

seasonal_wine.name: Riddling and disgorgement, year round
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: ongoing, after the legal ageing minimum
seasonal_wine.description: After the legal minimum of 15 months on lees for non-vintage and 36 months for vintage, bottles are riddled to gather the sediment in the neck, then disgorged to expel it before the final dosage. The disgorgement date increasingly appears on grower labels.
seasonal_wine.tip: Check for a disgorgement date on grower bottles; a recent date means fresher, more vibrant fizz in the glass.

seasonal_wine.name: Habits de Lumiere, December
seasonal_wine.description: In December the Avenue de Champagne in Epernay lights up for Habits de Lumiere, with illuminations, video mapping, fireworks, champagne bars and a vintage-car parade. It is the festive high point of the year on the most famous street in Champagne.
seasonal_wine.tip: Dress warm and arrive early on the Saturday evening to claim a spot on the avenue before the fireworks.

seasonal_wine.name: Festive season, December
seasonal_wine.description: December is when the world drinks the most Champagne, and the houses release their festive allocations and prestige cuvees for the holidays. Demand peaks for vintage and rose bottlings as the year closes.
seasonal_wine.tip: Buy the bottles you want for the holidays by late November; the best vintage and grower cuvees sell out before Christmas.
